# **Setting Up the Hardware**

**==WARNING: Before handling the Raspberry Pi or its camera, make sure you aren’t statically charged by touching [grounded metal](https://www.wikihow.com/Ground-Yourself). Static charge may damage the small components on the Raspberry Pi or its camera.==**

**The components on the Raspberry Pi and camera module are delicate, so handle them both with care.**

| Table of Contents | Related Documents |
|---|---|
| [Overview](#overview) | [Building a Creature-like, Face-Detection Robot Using Raspberry Pi (Home Page)](README.md) |
| [Assembling the “Head” of the Robot](#assembling-the-head-of-the-robot) | [Required Materials](required-materials.md) |
| [Wiring the Servos](#wiring-the-servos) | [Setting up the Raspberry Pi and the Raspberry Pi Camera](rasp-pi-set-up.md) |
| | [Setting up the Robot's Vision](robot-vision.md) |
| | [Setting up the "Ear Motors"](ear-motors.md) |
| | [Frequently Answered Questions](faq.md) |

## **Overview**

This tutorial is intended for anyone building this robot project, regardless of their prior experience. Since the hardware assembly is specific to this project's 3D printed parts and components, the steps below are designed to be followed exactly as written.

In this tutorial, you will assemble the robot's “head” by installing the servos, attaching the “ear” attachments, mounting the camera, and attaching the “head” stand. You will also set up the breadboard with the power module and wire the servos.

**Prerequisites:**

* Raspberry Pi 5   
* Raspberry Pi Module 3 Camera with no ribbon cable connected to it  
* Raspberry Pi camera case and its screws  
* Small Philips screwdriver  
* 3D printed parts (STL files can be found in the [Required Materials](required-materials.md) page and printed using a personal 3D printer, third-party printing service, or a local makerspace)
  * A “head” module
  * 2 “ear” attachments
  * A “head” stand
* 2 servos  
* 2 servo horns  
* Breadboard  
* Power module  
* Barrel jack power cable and brick  
* Jumper cables

## **Assembling the “Head” of the Robot**

1) **Install the servos into the “head” module by screwing them into place. Screw the servos in such that each servo sits flush with the surface of the part and does not wobble.**

![][image1]

2) **Once both servos are secured to the “head” module, snap the servo horns onto the servo shafts by pressing them into place.** 

![][image2]

It should look like this afterwards:  
![][image3]

3) **Snap the “ear” attachments onto the servo horns.**

There is an indent on the back-side of the attachments for the servo horn to click into place.

![][image4]

It should look like this afterwards:

![][image5]

4) **Align the camera case base with the camera and secure it using the screws and a small Phillips screwdriver.**

![][image6]

It should look like this afterwards:

![][image7]

5) **Attach the camera case lid to the base by snapping it into place.**

![][image8]

6) **Slide the camera within its case into slot in front of the “ear” attachments such that it looks like the below:**

![][image9]  
	

7) **Snap the “head” stand onto the bottom of the “head” piece, aligning the slots last seen in step 3 with the extrusions on the stand.**

![][image10]

The final product should look like the below:  
![][image11]![][image12]  
	  
Note: When attaching the ribbon cable to the camera, make sure the cable passes through the gap between the “head” stand’s legs like depicted in the right image above.

## **Wiring the Servos**

Now that the structure of the robot is set up, you have to connect the servos to a power source and the Raspberry Pi.

The servos operate on [Pulse-Width Modulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pulse-width_modulation) (PWM). PWM is the method the Raspberry Pi uses to send control signals to the servo motors. It works by rapidly switching a signal on and off, with the ratio of on-time to off-time telling the servo what angle to move to.

Each servo has three wires that follow standard color conventions for PWM:

* **Yellow (Signal)**: carries the PWM control signal from the Raspberry Pi  
* **Red (Power)**: provides 5V power to the servo motor  
* **Brown (Ground)**: completes the circuit by connecting to ground

The servos will receive signal output from the Raspberry Pi, while it receives power and a ground reference from a separate power source using a breadboard. You have to use a separate power source because if the servos are connected directly to a Raspberry Pi, the servos may draw too much power which may damage the Raspberry Pi in the process. 

**To set up the breadboard:** 

1) **Grab the power module, barrel head power cable and brick, and the breadboard. Place the power module in the upper half of the breadboard and insert the power module onto the breadboard such that its \+/- signs align with the breadboard’s positive and negative columns.** It should look like this:

![][image13]

This ensures that the breadboard’s positive and negative terminals accurately provide power and ground respectively.

2) **Move the connector under the barrel jack connector and the USB port such that the connector covers the 5V and center pin on the pins above the \+/- signs on the power module.**

This ensures the power provided by the breadboard’s positive terminals is 5V. If it’s over the center and 3.3V pin, it won’t provide enough power to the servos.

3) **Plug in the barrel head power cable and brick into the wall and into the barrel jack.** 

4) **Turn on the power module by pressing the on/off switch. Once the power module is on, the indicator LED light should turn green.**

**==WARNING: Leave the power module off while wiring the servos to prevent damage to components.==**

![][image14]
Photo by [Hackatronic](https://www.hackatronic.com/raspberry-pi-5-pinout-specifications-pricing-a-complete-guide/)

When wiring the servos, the signal wires will connect to GPIO pins. GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pins allow the Raspberry Pi to send and receive signals to external components like servos. Each pin has a specific number and purpose; some carry power, some carry ground, and some can send control signals like PWM. 

Refer to the labeled diagram of the pins above to know where to connect the signal wires to when following the instructions below.

**To connect your components together:**

**Connecting the Left “Ear” Servo**

1. Plug the left “ear” servo signal wire (yellow) into GPIO 14 on pin 8\.  
2. Plug the left “ear” servo ground wire into the leftmost negative (blue) side of the breadboard (anywhere along that column is acceptable).  
3. Plug the left “ear” servo power wire into on the leftmost positive (red) side of the breadboard (anywhere along that column is acceptable).

**Connecting the Right “Ear” Servo**

1. Plug the right “ear” servo signal wire (yellow) into GPIO 18 on pin 12\.  
2. Plug the right “ear” servo ground wire into the leftmost negative (blue) side of the breadboard (anywhere along that column is acceptable).  
3. Plug the right “ear” servo power wire on the leftmost positive (red) side of the breadboard (anywhere along that column is acceptable).

Once you’ve finished connecting everything together, your set up should look like this:

![][image15]

Once both servos are wired up, double-check that each wire is seated firmly in its pin and that no wires have been incorrectly wired. 

**==WARNING: Incorrect wiring can damage the Raspberry Pi or the servos.==**

Testing of this set up will be done once you reach [*Setting Up the “Ear” Motors*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1UGiC80ufGEso83c7PnFgwOVApgB1t09mBcOQ6ye1Nho/edit?tab=t.lipwx41lbn6j)*.*
